title: New York Fed Quarterly Report Shows Student Loan Debt Continues to Grow

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The NY Fed's Q1 2012 Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit reported student loan debt reached $904 billion, surpassing credit card debt as the second-largest form of consumer debt. The report documented ongoing consumer deleveraging with total household indebtedness declining to $11.44 trillion. Student loan 90+ day delinquency rates stood at 8.69%, higher than mortgages, auto loans, and HELOCs.
key_points: Student loan debt reached $904 billion in Q1 2012, up $30 billion from Q4 2011
Total household indebtedness declined to $11.44 trillion ($100 billion decrease from Q4 2011)
Student loans were the only consumer debt category increasing since household debt peaked in Q3 2008
90+ day student loan delinquency rate was 8.69%, higher than mortgages, auto loans, and HELOCs
Mortgage balances fell $81 billion; credit card balances at $679 billion (21.6% below Q4 2008 peak)
Published May 31, 2012 — nearly 14 years prior to TODAY (2026-04-01)
why_it_matters: This source is a historical household debt data release from 2012. While it documents important trends in consumer credit and deleveraging, it does not address market structure (trading venue operations, market making, order flow, clearing/settlement) or macro catalysts (policy decisions, economic data releases that move markets). The data is 14 years stale and has no actionable relevance for current market structure or macro catalyst research.
macro_context: This Q1 2012 report captures the post-financial crisis consumer deleveraging period. Student loan debt was the only debt category growing since the household debt peak in Q3 2008, while mortgages and credit cards were declining. The 90+ day student loan delinquency rate of 8.69% exceeded other debt categories. The data predates major student loan policy changes and the pandemic period.
